Tellius Incident Management Policy and
Procedure
Policy Owner: VP of Engineering
Effective Date: 01/01/2025
1. Purpose
This policy establishes a framework for managing security incidents that may affect the
confidentiality, integrity, or availability of Tellius's information assets and systems. It ensures
that incidents are:
Promptly identified.
Effectively contained.
Thoroughly investigated.
Appropriately eradicated.
Recovered from in a timely manner.
Documented and analyzed to prevent recurrence.
This policy aims to minimize the impact of security incidents, protect Tellius's reputation, and
comply with legal, regulatory, and contractual obligations, including the AICPA's Trust Services
Criteria for SOC 2.
2. Scope
This policy applies to all Tellius personnel, contractors, and third-party service providers who
use or have access to Tellius information systems and assets. It covers all types of security
incidents, including but not limited to:
Unauthorized access to systems or data
Data breaches or leaks
Malware infections
Denial-of-service attacks
Phishing attacks
Insider threats
Physical security breaches
Loss or theft of company devices containing sensitive information
3. Policy Statements
Incident Identification: Tellius identifies and classifies security incidents through proactive
monitoring, user reporting, and other detection mechanisms.
Incident Response Plan: A comprehensive Incident Response Plan �IRP) is maintained and
regularly tested to provide a structured approach to managing security incidents.
Incident Response Team: A designated Incident Response Team �IRT) is responsible for
coordinating and executing the incident response process.
Containment, Eradication, and Recovery: Incidents are contained, eradicated, and systems are
recovered in a timely manner to minimize damage and disruption.
Incident Documentation and Reporting: All security incidents are thoroughly documented, and
significant incidents are reported to relevant stakeholders, including regulatory bodies if
required.
1

Post-Incident Analysis: A post-incident analysis is conducted for all significant incidents to
determine the root cause, identify areas for improvement, and prevent recurrence.
Legal and Regulatory Compliance: Incident management activities comply with all applicable
legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements.
4. Procedures
Incident Identification
Monitoring and Detection:
Tellius employs security monitoring tools and techniques to detect potential security
incidents. These include, but are not limited to:
Security Information and Event Management �SIEM) systems
Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems �IDS/IPS�
Log analysis
Vulnerability scanning
System and application logs are regularly reviewed for suspicious activity.
User Reporting:
All Tellius personnel are required to report any suspected security incidents or
vulnerabilities immediately to the Security Team or designated contact.
Clear reporting mechanisms, such as a dedicated email address or hotline, are
provided.
Third-Party Reporting:
Procedures are established for receiving and processing security incident reports
from external parties, such as customers, vendors, and security researchers.
Incident Response Plan �IRP�
IRP Development and Maintenance:
The IRP is developed, maintained, and regularly updated by the Security Team.
The IRP includes detailed procedures for each phase of the incident response
lifecycle.
IRP Testing:
The IRP is tested regularly through simulations, tabletop exercises, or full-scale
exercises to ensure its effectiveness and identify areas for improvement.
Testing results are documented, and the IRP is updated as needed.
IRP Communication:
The IRP is communicated to all relevant personnel, and their roles and
responsibilities are clearly defined.
Incident Response Team �IRT�
IRT Composition:
The IRT comprises individuals with the necessary skills and expertise to handle
security incidents, including:
Security analysts
System administrators
Network engineers
Legal counsel
Public relations
Management representatives
IRT Roles and Responsibilities:
The IRT has clearly defined roles and responsibilities, including:
Incident lead
Containment specialist
Forensics analyst
Communications officer
IRT Communication:
2

The IRT has established communication channels and procedures for internal and
external stakeholders.
Containment, Eradication, and Recovery
Containment:
Incidents are contained to prevent further damage and limit their scope. Containment
measures may include:
Isolating affected systems or networks
Blocking malicious traffic
Taking systems offline
The containment strategy is documented.
Eradication:
The root cause of the incident is identified and eliminated to prevent recurrence.
Eradication may involve:
Removing malware
Patching vulnerabilities
Correcting misconfigurations
Eradication steps are documented.
Recovery:
Affected systems and data are recovered to normal operations as quickly and
securely as possible. Recovery procedures may include:
Restoring from backups
Rebuilding systems
Data recovery
The recovery process is documented.
Incident Documentation and Reporting
Incident Documentation:
All security incidents are documented in a centralized incident management system.
Documentation includes:
Date and time of the incident
Description of the incident
Affected systems and data
Incident severity and priority
Actions taken for containment, eradication, and recovery
Root cause analysis
Lessons learned
Evidence related to the incident is preserved.
Internal Reporting:
Incident reports are provided to relevant internal stakeholders, including:
Management
Legal counsel
Affected system owners
External Reporting:
Significant security incidents are reported to external parties as required by law,
regulation, or contract. This may include:
Customers
Law enforcement
Regulatory agencies
Data protection authorities
Post-Incident Analysis
Analysis:
A post-incident analysis is conducted for all significant incidents to determine the
underlying causes and contributing factors.
Corrective Actions:
3

Corrective actions are identified and implemented to prevent similar incidents from
occurring in the future. These actions may include:
Improving security controls
Enhancing monitoring and detection capabilities
Providing additional security training
Modifying policies and procedures
Documentation:
The post-incident analysis findings and corrective actions are documented.
5. Roles and Responsibilities
All Personnel:
Responsible for reporting suspected security incidents immediately.
Incident Response Team �IRT��
Responsible for managing and coordinating the response to security incidents according
to the IRP.
Conducting incident analysis and investigations.
Implementing containment, eradication, and recovery measures.
Communicating with stakeholders.
Documenting incident details.
Security Team:
Developing and maintaining the IRP.
Providing guidance and support to the IRT.
Conducting post-incident analysis.
Implementing corrective actions.
Ensuring compliance with legal and regulatory requirements.
IT Operations:
Assisting the IRT with technical aspects of incident response, such as system isolation
and recovery.
Implementing security controls and configurations to prevent incidents.
Providing system logs and other relevant data.
Legal Counsel:
Providing legal advice on incident response activities and reporting requirements.
Liaising with law enforcement and regulatory agencies as needed.
Management:
Providing resources and support for incident management activities.
Ensuring that the incident response plan is regularly tested and updated.
Ensuring compliance with this policy.
6. Compliance
6.1 Policy Compliance: All Tellius personnel and third parties are required to comply with this
policy.
6.2 Non-Compliance: Non-compliance with this policy may result in disciplinary action, up to
and including termination of employment or contracts, and potential legal consequences.
6.3 Policy Review: This policy and the associated IRP will be reviewed and updated at least
annually or as needed to reflect changes in technology, threats, and business requirements.

Information Security Policy
Policy Owner: VP of Engineering
Effective Date: 01/01/2025
Overview
This Information Security Policy is intended to protect Tellius, Inc.'s employees, partners and the
company from illegal or damaging actions by individuals, either knowingly or unknowingly.
Internet/Intranet/Extranet-related systems, including but not limited to computer equipment,
software, operating systems, storage media, network accounts providing electronic mail, web
browsing, and file transfers, are the property of Tellius, Inc.. These systems are to be used for
business purposes in serving the interests of the company, and of our clients and customers in the
course of normal operations.
Effective security is a team effort involving the participation and support of every Tellius, Inc.
employee or contractor who deals with information and/or information systems. It is the
responsibility of every team member to read and understand this policy, and to conduct their
activities accordingly.
Purpose
The purpose of this policy is to communicate our information security policies and outline the
acceptable use and protection of Tellius, Inc.'s information and assets. These rules are in place to
protect customers, employees, and Tellius, Inc.. Inappropriate use exposes Tellius, Inc. to risks
including virus attacks, compromise of network systems and services, and legal and compliance
issues.
The Tellius, Inc. "Information Security Policy" is comprised of this policy and all Tellius, Inc. policies
referenced and/or linked within this document.
Scope
This policy applies to the use of information, electronic and computing devices, and network
resources to conduct Tellius, Inc. business or interact with internal networks and business systems,
whether owned or leased by Tellius, Inc., the employee, or a third party. All employees, contractors,
consultants, temporary, and other workers at Tellius, Inc. and its subsidiaries are responsible for
exercising good judgment regarding appropriate use of information, electronic devices, and network
resources in accordance with Tellius, Inc. policies and standards, and local laws and regulations.
This policy applies to employees, contractors, consultants, temporaries, and other workers at Tellius,
Inc., including all personnel affiliated with third parties. This policy applies to all Tellius, Inc.-
controlled company and customer data as well as all equipment, systems, networks and software
owned or leased by Tellius, Inc..
Security Incident Reporting
All users are required to report known or suspected security events or incidents, including policy
violations and observed security weaknesses. Incidents should be reported immediately or as soon
1

as possible by email to hroperations@tellius.com. In your email please describe the incident or
observation along with any relevant details.
Mobile Device Policy
Tellius Inc. enforces this policy on all end-user devices, including mobile phones, tablets, laptops,
and desktops, used to access company information systems. The following security standards are in
place to govern the use of these devices, whether company-owned or personally owned �BYOD��
Email Security: Users are required to exercise extreme caution when opening email
attachments, especially those from unknown or untrusted senders, as these may contain
malware. Tellius security systems and user training emphasize this requirement.
Password Protection: All system-level and user-level passwords must comply with the Tellius
Access Control Policy. Sharing of login credentials or failure to secure device access is strictly
prohibited and actively prevented through technical controls and user agreements.
Device Security Requirements: All devices used to access Tellius, Inc. information systems,
including company email, adhere to the following security standards:
Device Encryption: Devices must be encrypted with a password-protected screensaver or
screen lock. This is enforced through device configuration policies.
Device Lockout: Devices are configured to lock automatically whenever left unattended.
Incident Reporting: Users are required to immediately report any suspected misuse or theft of a
mobile device to the Operations Director. This reporting process is communicated to all users.
Data Storage: Storage of confidential information on mobile devices or USB drives is
prohibited. Technical controls and user agreements enforce this restriction. Basic business
contact information, such as names, phone numbers, and email addresses, is exempt from this
restriction.
Device Sharing: Sharing of any mobile device used to access company email with any other
person is strictly prohibited and actively prevented.
Termination Procedures: Upon termination of employment or a contract, the following
procedures are enforced:
1. Return of Devices: Users must return all company-owned devices.
2. Data Removal: Users are required to remove all company information and accounts from any
personal devices. Acknowledgement of this requirement is obtained during the off-boarding
process.
Remote Access Policy
Laptops and other computer resources that are used to access the Tellius, Inc. network must
conform to the security requirements outlined in Tellius, Inc.'s Information Security Policies and
adhere to the following standards:
To ensure mobile devices do not connect a compromised device to the company network,
Antivirus policies require the use and enforcement of client-side antivirus software
Users are prohibited from changing or disabling any organizational security controls such as
personal firewalls, antivirus software on systems used to access Tellius, Inc. resources
Use of remote access software and/or services (e.g., VPN client) is allowable as long as it is
provided by the company and configured for multifactor authentication �MFA�
Unauthorized remote access technologies may not be used or installed on any Tellius, Inc.
system
Whenever possible users should use a VPN when transmitting confidential information on
public Wi-Fi
2

If you access from a public computer (e.g., business center, hotel, etc.), log out of session and
don't save anything. Don't check "remember me, collect all printed materials and delete
downloaded files (generally is discouraged)
Removable Media and Portable Storage Devices
The company prohibits the use of removable media (e.g., USB drives, external hard drives, SD cards,
or other portable storage devices) for storing or transferring company data, unless explicitly
authorized by the Information Security team.
Encryption Requirement: Any authorized use of removable media must ensure that the device is
fully encrypted using company-approved encryption standards.
Blocking Policy: By default, company-managed devices are configured to block or restrict the use of
removable media to prevent unauthorized data transfer.
Exceptions: In rare cases where removable media use is required for business purposes, prior
written approval from the Information Security team must be obtained, and encryption must be
enforced.
User Responsibility: Employees must not copy, transfer, or store company data on personal or
unencrypted removable media devices under any circumstances.
Monitoring and Enforcement: The company reserves the right to monitor device usage and enforce
technical controls to prevent unauthorized use of removable media. Violations of this policy may
result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination.
Acceptable Use Policy
Tellius, Inc. proprietary and customer information stored on electronic and computing devices
whether owned or leased by Tellius, Inc., the employee or a third party, remains the sole property of
Tellius, Inc. for the purposes of this policy. Employees and contractors must ensure through legal or
technical means that proprietary information is protected in accordance with the Data Management
Policy. The use of <company file sharing system, e.g., Google Drive> for business file storage is
required for users of laptops or company issued devices. Storing important documents on the file
share is how you "backup" your laptop.
You have a responsibility to promptly report the theft, loss, or unauthorized disclosure of Tellius, Inc.
proprietary information. You may access, use or share Tellius, Inc. proprietary information only to the
extent it is authorized and necessary to fulfill your assigned job duties. Employees are responsible
for exercising good judgment regarding the reasonableness of personal use of company-provided
devices.
For security and network maintenance purposes, authorized individuals within Tellius, Inc. may
monitor equipment, systems and network traffic at any time.
Tellius, Inc. reserves the right to audit networks and systems on a periodic basis to ensure
compliance with this policy.
Unacceptable Use
The following activities are, in general, prohibited. Employees may be exempted from these
restrictions during the course of their legitimate job responsibilities. Under no circumstances is an
employee of Tellius, Inc. authorized to engage in any activity that is illegal under local, state, federal
3

or international law while utilizing Tellius, Inc.-owned resources. The list below is not exhaustive, but
attempts to provide a framework for activities which fall into the category of unacceptable use.
The following activities are strictly prohibited, with no exceptions:
���Violations of the rights of any person or company protected by copyright, trade secret, patent,
or other intellectual property, or similar laws or regulations, including, but not limited to, the
installation or distribution of "pirated" or other software products that are not appropriately

���Unauthorized copying of copyrighted material including, but not limited to, digitization and

���Revealing your account password to others or allowing use of your account by others. This
includes family and other household members when work is being done at home.

���Making fraudulent offers of products, items, or services originating from any Tellius, Inc.

or size of messages.

Operations Security Policy
Policy Owner: Operations Lead
Effective Date: 02/01/2023
���Purpose
To ensure the correct and secure operation of information processing systems and facilities.
���Scope
All Tellius, Inc. information systems that are business critical and/or process, store, or transmit
company data. This Policy applies to all employees of Tellius, Inc. and other third-party entities with
access to Tellius, Inc. networks and system resources.
���Operations Security
Documented Operating Procedures
Operating procedures shall be documented and made available to all users who need them via
GDrive documents or Notion
Change Management
Changes to the organization, business processes, information processing facilities, and systems that
affect information security in the production environment and financial systems shall be controlled.
All significant changes to in-scope systems must be documented.
Change management processes shall include: �Change Management Process]
Processes for planning and testing of changes, including remediation measures
Documented managerial approval and authorization before proceeding with changes that may
have a significant impact on information security, operations, or the production platform
Advance communication/warning of changes, including schedules and a description of
reasonably anticipated effects, provided to all relevant internal and external stakeholders
Documentation of all emergency changes and subsequent review
A process for remediating unsuccessful changes
Capacity Management
The use of processing resources and system storage shall be monitored and adjusted to ensure that
system availability and performance meets Tellius, Inc. requirements.
Human resource skills, availability, and capacity shall be reviewed and considered as a component
of capacity planning and as part of the annual risk assessment process.
Scaling resources for additional processing or storage capacity, without changes to the system, can
be done outside of the standard change management and code deployment process.
Separation of Development, Staging and Production Environments
Development and staging environments shall be strictly segregated from production SaaS
environments to reduce the risks of unauthorized access or changes to the operational environment.
Confidential production customer data must not be used in development or test environments
without the express approval of the Engineering Leads.
1

For a full description, see the Data Management Policy for a description of Confidential data. If
production customer data is approved for use in the course of development or testing, it shall be
scrubbed of any such sensitive information whenever feasible.
���Systems and Network Configuration, Hardening, and Review
Systems and networks shall be provisioned and maintained in accordance with the configuration and
hardening standards in Appendix A to this policy.
Firewalls shall be used to control network traffic to and from the production environment in
accordance with this policy.
Production firewall rules shall be reviewed at least annually. Tickets shall be created to obtain
approvals for any needed changes.
���Protection from Malware
In order to protect the company's infrastructure against the introduction of malicious software,
detection, prevention, and recovery controls to protect against malware shall be implemented,
combined with appropriate user awareness.
Anti-malware protections shall be utilized on all employee issued laptops except for those running
operating systems not normally prone to malicious software (example Macbooks Pro). Additionally,
threat detection and response software shall be utilized for company email. The anti-malware
protections utilized shall be capable of detecting all common forms of malicious threats.
Tellius, Inc. should scan all files upon their introduction to systems, and continually scan files upon
access, modification, or download. Anti-malware definition updates should be configured to be
downloaded and installed automatically whenever new updates are available. Known or suspected
malware incidents must be reported as a security incident.
It is a violation of company policy to disable or alter the configuration of anti-malware protections
without authorization.
���Information Backup
The need for backups of systems, databases, information and data shall be considered and
appropriate backup processes shall be designed, planned and implemented. Security measures to
protect backups shall be designed and applied in accordance with the confidentiality or sensitivity of
the data. Backup copies of information, software and system images shall be taken regularly to
protect against loss of data. Backups and restore capabilities shall be periodically tested, not less
than annually.
Tellius, Inc. does not regularly backup user devices like laptops. Users are expected to store critical
files and information in company-sanctioned file storage repositories �GDrive, S3 Buckets in AWS or
OneDrive).
Backups are configured to run twice daily on in-scope systems. The backup schedules are
maintained within the backup application software.
���Logging & Monitoring
Production infrastructure shall be configured to produce detailed logs appropriate to the function
served by the system or device. Event logs recording user activities, exceptions, faults and
information security events shall be produced, kept and reviewed through manual or automated
processes as needed. Appropriate alerts shall be configured for events that represent a significant
threat to the confidentiality, availability or integrity of production systems or Confidential data.
2

Protection of Log Information
Logging facilities and log information shall be protected against tampering and unauthorized access.
Administrator & Operator Logs
System administrator and system operator activities shall be logged and reviewed and/or alerted in
accordance with the system classification and criticality.
Clock Synchronization
The clocks of all relevant information processing systems within an organization or security domain
shall be synchronized to network time servers using reputable time sources.
���Control of Operational Software
The installation of software on production systems shall follow the change management
requirements defined in this policy.
���Technical Vulnerability Management
Information about technical vulnerabilities of information systems being used shall be obtained in a
timely fashion, the organization's exposure to such vulnerabilities shall be evaluated, and appropriate
measures taken to address the associated risk. A variety of methods shall be used to obtain
information about technical vulnerabilities, including vulnerability scanning, penetration tests, and
the bug bounty program.
External vulnerability scans shall be run on the production environment at least quarterly. Interior
vulnerability scans shall be run against test environments which mirror production configurations.
Penetration tests of the applications and production network shall be performed at least annually.
Additional scanning and testing shall be performed following major changes to production systems.
The IT and Engineering departments shall evaluate the severity of vulnerabilities, and if it is
determined to be a critical or high-risk vulnerability, a service ticket will be created. The Tellius, Inc.
assessed severity level may differ from the level automatically generated by scanning software or
determined by external researchers based on Tellius, Inc.'s internal knowledge and understanding of
technical architecture and real-world impact/exploitability. Tickets are assigned to the system,
application, or platform owners for further investigation and/or remediation.
Vulnerabilities assessed by Tellius, Inc. shall be remediated in the following timeframes:
Determined SeverityRemediation Time
Critical 30 Days
High 30 Days
Medium 60 Day
Low 90 Days
Informational As needed
Service tickets for any vulnerability which cannot be remediated within the standard timeline must
show a risk treatment plan and planned remediation timeline.
���Restrictions on Software Installation
Rules governing the installation of software by users shall be established and implemented in
accordance with the Tellius, Inc. Information Security Policy.
3

���Information Systems Audit Considerations
Audit requirements and activities involving verification of operational systems shall be carefully
planned and agreed to minimize disruptions to business processes.
���Exceptions
Requests for an exception to this policy must be submitted to the Operations Lead for approval.
���Violations & Enforcement
Any known violations of this policy should be reported to the Operations Lead. Violations of this
policy can result in immediate withdrawal or suspension of system and network privileges and/or
disciplinary action in accordance with company procedures up to and including termination of
employment.

Tellius Risk and Vulnerability Assessment
Program
Policy Owner: VP of Engineering
Effective Date: 01/01/2025
1. Purpose
This document outlines Tellius's program for managing risks and vulnerabilities related to its
information systems and assets. It establishes a framework for:
Identifying, analyzing, and evaluating risks to Tellius's information systems.
Identifying, analyzing, and evaluating vulnerabilities in Tellius's information systems.
Implementing appropriate controls to mitigate risks and address vulnerabilities.
Ensuring compliance with relevant legal, regulatory, and contractual obligations, including
the AICPA's Trust Services Criteria for SOC 2.
2. Scope
This program applies to all Tellius information systems and assets, including but not limited to:
Applications
Systems
Networks
Infrastructure
Data
Hardware
Software
Facilities
Personnel
3. Policy Statements
3.1 Risk Assessments:
Risk assessments are conducted regularly to identify, analyze, and evaluate potential
threats and vulnerabilities to Tellius's information systems.
Risk assessments are performed for every release which is every 8 weeks.
These assessments consider the likelihood and potential impact of identified risks.
Risk assessments are used to prioritize and guide the implementation of security controls.
3.2 Vulnerability Assessments:
Vulnerability assessments are conducted to identify, analyze, and evaluate technical
weaknesses in Tellius's information systems.
These assessments employ a combination of automated and manual techniques.
Identified vulnerabilities are promptly reported to system owners and remediated in a
timely manner.
3.3 Remediation:
Remediation plan
Critical - 2 weeks
High - 4 weeks
Medium - 8 weeks
Low - 90 days
1

A well-defined process is in place to address identified risks and vulnerabilities.
Remediation activities are prioritized based on the severity of the risk or vulnerability.
The effectiveness of remediation activities is verified.
3.4 Documentation:
All risk and vulnerability assessments, and remediation activities are thoroughly
documented.
This documentation is maintained and kept up-to-date.
3.5 Roles and Responsibilities:
Roles and responsibilities for conducting risk and vulnerability assessments, and for
implementing remediation activities, are clearly defined and assigned.
3.6 Frequency:
Risk and vulnerability assessments are performed with a frequency appropriate to the
environment and business needs.
4. Procedures
4.1 Risk Assessment Procedures
4.1.1 Risk Identification:
Identify potential threats (e.g., unauthorized access, data breach, natural disaster)
and vulnerabilities (e.g., software flaws, misconfigurations) that could affect
information systems.
Consider internal and external threats.
4.1.2 Risk Analysis:
Evaluate the likelihood of each threat occurring.
Assign vulnerabilities to relevant asset owners or teams
Determine the potential impact (e.g., financial loss, reputational damage, legal
consequences) if the threat were to be realized.
4.1.3 Risk Evaluation:
Prioritize identified risks based on their likelihood and impact.
Use a risk matrix or other methodology to assign risk levels (e.g., high, medium, low).
4.1.4 Risk Treatment:
Determine appropriate risk treatment options:
Mitigation: Implement controls to reduce the likelihood or impact of the risk.
Transfer: Transfer the risk to a third party (e.g., insurance).
Acceptance: Accept the risk if the cost of mitigation outweighs the potential
benefit.
Avoidance: Eliminate the risk by not engaging in the activity.
Document the selected risk treatment strategy.
4.1.5 Documentation:
Document the risk assessment process, including:
Scope of the assessment
Identified threats and vulnerabilities
Likelihood and impact analysis
Risk levels
Risk treatment decisions
Responsible parties
Review and approval
4.1.6 Schedule:
Define the schedule for risk assessments (e.g., annually, semi-annually, or more
frequently based on the environment).
4.2 Vulnerability Assessment Procedures
4.2.1 Vulnerability Scanning:
2

Perform regular vulnerability scans of systems, networks, and applications using
automated tools.
Select appropriate scanning tools based on the target systems and applications.
Configure scanning tools to check for a wide range of vulnerabilities (e.g., CVEs).
Perform both authenticated and unauthenticated scans.
4.2.2 Penetration Testing:
Conduct periodic penetration testing (every 6 months) to simulate real-world attacks
and identify exploitable vulnerabilities.
Use qualified and experienced penetration testers (internal or external).
Define the scope and rules of engagement for penetration tests.
Perform penetration testing on critical systems and applications.
4.2.3 Manual Vulnerability Assessments:
Perform manual vulnerability assessments, such as:
Code reviews: Reviewing source code for security flaws.
Configuration reviews: Checking system and application configurations for
security weaknesses.
Security audits: Evaluating security controls and processes.
4.2.4 Vulnerability Analysis:
Analyze the results of vulnerability scans and penetration tests to identify true
vulnerabilities and prioritize them.
Determine the potential impact and exploitability of each vulnerability.
4.2.5 Reporting:
Document and report identified vulnerabilities, including:
Description of the vulnerability
Affected systems and applications
Severity level (e.g., critical, high, medium, low)
Recommendations for remediation
4.2.6 Schedule:
Define the schedule for vulnerability assessments:
Vulnerability scanning: (e.g., weekly, monthly)
Penetration testing: (e.g., annually, semi-annually)
Manual vulnerability assessments: (e.g., as needed, triggered by significant
changes)
4.3 Remediation Procedures
4.3.1 Vulnerability Prioritization:
Prioritize vulnerabilities for remediation based on:
Severity level
Exploitability
Potential impact
Business criticality of affected systems
4.3.2 Remediation Planning:
Develop a remediation plan that includes:
Specific actions to be taken
Responsible parties
Timelines for remediation
Required resources
Testing and verification procedures
4.3.3 Remediation Implementation:
Implement remediation actions according to the remediation plan.
Apply patches, update configurations, or implement other necessary changes.
4.3.4 Verification:
Verify the effectiveness of remediation actions through:
Re-scanning with vulnerability scanning tools
Re-testing with penetration testing
3

Manual verification
4.3.5 Exceptions:
Document any exceptions to the remediation process.
Exceptions must be approved by appropriate authority.
4.3.6 Tracking:
Track the progress of remediation activities.
Monitor remediation timelines.
Escalate overdue remediation.
5. Roles and Responsibilities
5.1 System Owners:
Responsible for the security of their systems.
Ensure that vulnerability assessments are performed.
Implement remediation actions for identified vulnerabilities.
5.2 Security Team:
Develop and maintain this program.
Conduct or oversee risk and vulnerability assessments.
Provide guidance and support on risk and vulnerability management.
Track and report on remediation activities.
Perform penetration testing.
5.3 IT Operations:
Assist with vulnerability scanning and remediation.
Implement system and network configurations that support security.
Apply patches and updates in a timely manner.
5.4 Application Development Teams:
Ensure that applications are developed with security in mind.
Participate in code reviews and security testing.
Address vulnerabilities identified during testing.
5.5 Management:
Provide resources and support for the risk and vulnerability assessment program.
Ensure that risk and vulnerability management activities are integrated into the
organization's overall security program.
Ensure compliance with this program.
6. Compliance
6.1 Program Compliance: All Tellius personnel are required to comply with this program.
6.2 Non-Compliance: Non-compliance with this program may result in disciplinary action, up to
and including termination of employment or contracts, and potential legal consequences.
6.3 Program Review: This program will be reviewed and updated at least annually or as needed
to reflect changes in technology, threats, vulnerabilities, and business requirements.

1. Purpose
This policy establishes the Software Development Lifecycle �SDLC) and Application Security
practices for Tellius. It provides a framework for the secure and controlled development,
testing, and deployment of software applications and systems to:
Ensure the security, reliability, and quality of all software developed, acquired, or
maintained by Tellius.
Protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of Tellius data and systems.
Comply with relevant regulatory requirements, industry standards, and best practices,
including the AICPA's Trust Services Criteria for SOC 2.
Establish a consistent, repeatable, and secure process for software development.
2. Scope
This policy applies to all Tellius personnel and any external parties involved in the software
development lifecycle, including but not limited to:
Software Engineers �Front-End, Back-End)
Data Engineers
Machine Learning Engineers
Product Managers
Designers
Quality Assurance �QA� Engineers
DevOps Engineers
Security Engineers
Contractors
Third-party vendors involved in software development or integration
This policy encompasses all software applications, systems, and services developed, acquired,
or maintained by Tellius, including:
Tellius application
APIs and SDKs
Cloud infrastructure and services
Data processing pipelines
Machine learning models and related code
Internal tools and systems
3. Policy Statements
Secure SDLC Framework: Tellius follows a structured SDLC framework that incorporates
security best practices into each phase. The SDLC phases include:
Planning
Design
Development
Testing
1

Deployment
Maintenance
Security by Design: Security is a core consideration throughout the SDLC, not an add-on.
Security requirements are identified, documented, and addressed in the initial planning and
design phases.
Secure Coding Practices: All software development adheres to secure coding practices and
guidelines to minimize the introduction of vulnerabilities.
Testing and Quality Assurance: Rigorous testing and quality assurance processes, including
security testing, are conducted to verify software functionality, reliability, and security.
Change Management: All software changes are managed through a controlled change
management process to ensure that changes are authorized, tested, and implemented in a
secure and documented manner.
Vulnerability Management: A proactive vulnerability management program is in place to
identify, assess, and remediate software vulnerabilities in a timely manner.
Access Control: Access to development environments, code repositories, build systems, and
deployment systems is restricted based on the principle of least privilege and job
responsibilities.
Data Security: Software development activities involving sensitive data adhere to data security
policies and procedures, including data encryption, data masking, and data loss prevention
measures.
Training and Awareness: All personnel involved in the SDLC receive regular security training
and awareness education to stay updated on secure development practices and security
threats.
Incident Response: A security incident response plan is in place to address and manage
security incidents that may arise during the software development lifecycle.
4. Procedures
Planning
Requirements Analysis:
Functional and non-functional requirements, including security requirements, are
clearly defined, documented, and approved by relevant stakeholders.
Security requirements are derived from applicable laws, regulations, industry
standards (e.g., OWASP�, and risk assessments.
Data Privacy, Security risks are clearly identified and documented.
Risk Assessment:
Security risk assessments are conducted to identify potential threats and
vulnerabilities early in the SDLC.
Risk assessments consider factors such as data sensitivity, system criticality, and
potential impact.
Project Planning:
Project plans include security tasks, timelines, and resource allocation.
Security activities are integrated into the overall project schedule.
Design
Secure Design Principles:
Software is designed using secure design principles, including:
Least privilege: Granting only the minimum necessary permissions.
Defense in depth: Implementing multiple layers of security controls.
Fail-safe defaults: Defaulting to a secure state.
Separation of duties: Dividing responsibilities to prevent single points of failure.
Principle of least astonishment
Resiliency: Design systems to be resilient and recover quickly from failure.
Threat Modeling:
2

Threat modeling is performed to identify potential attack vectors, vulnerabilities, and
the impact of potential threats.
Threat modeling informs the design of security controls and testing strategies.
Security Architecture Review:
Security architecture reviews are conducted to evaluate the design for potential
security weaknesses.
Reviews are performed by qualified security personnel or architects.
API Security:
APIs are designed with security as a priority, implementing authentication,
authorization, input validation, and rate limiting.
Development
Secure Coding Standards:
Developers adhere to secure coding standards and guidelines (e.g., OWASP Secure
Coding Practices, CERT Coding Standards) for all programming languages used.
Code examples:
Input validation: All user-supplied input is validated to prevent injection attacks.
Output encoding: Data is properly encoded to prevent cross-site scripting
�XSS�.
Error handling: Errors are handled securely without exposing sensitive
information.
Authentication and authorization: Secure mechanisms are used to verify user
identity and enforce access control.
Code Reviews:
Peer code reviews are conducted to identify potential security flaws and ensure
adherence to secure coding standards.
Code reviewers are trained in secure coding practices.
Static Application Security Testing �SAST��
SAST tools are used to automatically analyze source code for potential
vulnerabilities.
SAST tools are integrated into the CI/CD pipeline where possible.
Dependency Management:
Software dependencies are managed using a centralized system.
Dependencies are regularly scanned for known vulnerabilities.
Only approved and trusted libraries and frameworks are used.
Logging:
Security events, errors, and warnings are logged to detect potential security issues.
Logged data is protected to prevent unauthorized access.
Testing
Security Testing:
Security testing is performed to verify the effectiveness of security controls and
identify vulnerabilities.
Security testing includes:
Vulnerability scanning: Automated scanning for known vulnerabilities.
Dynamic Application Security Testing �DAST�� Testing the application in a
running state to find vulnerabilities.
Penetration testing: Simulating real-world attacks to identify weaknesses.
Fuzz testing: Providing invalid, unexpected, or random data as inputs.
Manual code review: In-depth analysis of the code to find vulnerabilities that
automated tools might miss.
Test Environment:
A separate and secure test environment is maintained for conducting testing.
Test data is anonymized or synthetic to protect sensitive information.
Test Planning:
3

Test plans include security test cases that cover identified security risks and
requirements.
Regression Testing:
Regression tests are performed to ensure that new changes do not negatively impact
existing functionality or security.
QA Sign-off:
The QA team is responsible for verifying that software meets both functional and
security requirements before release.
Deployment
Change Management Process:
All software deployments follow a documented and approved change management
process.
The change management process includes:
Request for change �RFC) submission.
Impact analysis.
Testing and approval.
Backout plan.
Post-deployment validation.
Deployment Environment Security:
Deployment environments are secured to prevent unauthorized access.
Access to production systems is strictly controlled and audited.
Deployment Procedures:
Detailed deployment procedures are documented and followed.
Deployments are automated where possible to reduce manual errors and improve
consistency.
Post-Deployment Validation:
Post-deployment security checks are performed to verify that the software is
deployed securely and functioning as intended.
Maintenance
Vulnerability Management:
Production systems and software are regularly scanned for vulnerabilities.
A process is in place to track, prioritize, and remediate identified vulnerabilities
based on severity.
Security patches are applied in a timely manner following a defined schedule.
Security Monitoring:
Security monitoring and logging are implemented to detect and respond to security
events.
Intrusion detection and prevention systems �IDPS) are used where appropriate.
Incident Response:
A security incident response plan is in place to handle security incidents, including:
Identification
Containment
Eradication
Recovery
Post-incident analysis
Software Updates:
Software is updated regularly to patch security vulnerabilities and ensure
compatibility.
5. Responsibilities
Software Engineers:
Adhere to secure coding practices and development standards.
4

Participate in code reviews and address identified security vulnerabilities.
Perform unit tests, and contribute to integration and system testing.
Document code and development processes.
QA Engineers:
Develop and execute test plans, including security test cases.
Report and track identified defects and vulnerabilities.
Verify that software meets both functional and security requirements.
Participate in risk assessment and threat modeling.
DevOps Engineers:
Establish and maintain secure development, testing, and production environments.
Implement security controls in the CI/CD pipeline.
Automate security testing and deployment processes.
Manage and monitor infrastructure security.
Security Engineers:
Develop and maintain security policies, standards, and procedures.
Provide security training and guidance to development teams.
Conduct security assessments, penetration tests, and vulnerability scans.
Participate in incident response and security audits.
Perform threat modeling.
Product Managers:
Incorporate security requirements into product planning and roadmaps.
Prioritize security features and bug fixes.
Ensure that development teams have the resources and support needed to develop
secure software.
Management:
Provide adequate resources and budget for security activities.
Promote a culture of security awareness and responsibility.
Ensure that all personnel are aware of and comply with this policy.
Support the implementation and enforcement of this policy.
6. Compliance
Policy Compliance: All Tellius personnel and third parties are required to comply with this
policy.
Non-Compliance: Non-compliance with this policy may result in disciplinary action, up to and
including termination of employment or contracts, and potential legal consequences.

and to all external parties, including but not limitedtoTellius, Inc.consultants, contractors,
business partners, vendors, suppliers, partners, outsourcedservice providers, and other
third-party entities with access toTellius, Inc.data, systems, networks, or system resources.
Policy
3.
Information security requirements for mitigating therisks associated with supplier's access to
the organization's assets shall be agreed with thesupplier and documented.
For all service providers who may accessTellius,Inc.Confidential data, systems, or networks,
proper due diligence shall be performed prior to provisioningaccess or engaging in processing
activities. Information shall be maintained regardingwhich regulatory or certification
requirements are managed by or impacted by each serviceprovider, and which are managed by
Tellius, Inc.as required. Applicable regulatory orcertification requirements may include ISO
27001, SOC 2, PCI-DSS, CCPA, GDPR or other frameworksor regulations.
1

Information Security in Third-Party Relationships
4.
Addressing Security in Agreements
Relevant information security requirements shall beestablished and agreed with each supplier
that may access, process, store, or transmit Confidential data, or provide physical or virtual IT
infrastructure components forTellius, Inc..
For all service providers who may accessTellius,Inc.production systems, or who may impact
the security of theTellius, Inc.production environment,written agreements shall be maintained
that include the service provider's acknowledgmentof their responsibilities for the
confidentiality of company and customer data, andany commitments regarding the integrity,
availability, and/or privacy controls that they managein order to meet the standards and
requirements thatTellius, Inc.has established inaccordance withTellius, Inc.’s information
security program or any relevant framework.
Technology Supply Chain
Tellius, Inc.will consider and assess risk associatedwith suppliers and the technology supply
chain. Where warranted, agreements with suppliersshall include requirements to address the
relevant information security risks associated withinformation and communications technology
services and the product supply chain.
Third-Party Service Delivery Management
5.
Monitoring & Review of Third-Party Services
Tellius, Inc.shall regularly monitor, review, andaudit supplier service delivery. Supplier security
and service delivery performance shall be reviewedat least annually.
Management of Changes to Third-Party Services
Changes to the provision of services by suppliers,including changes to agreements, services,
technology, policies, procedures, or controls, shallbe managed, taking account of the criticality
of the business information, systems, and processesinvolved.Tellius, Inc.shall assess the risk of
any material changes made by suppliers and make appropriatemodifications to agreements and
services accordingly.
2

Third-Party Risk Management
6.
Tellius, Inc.will ensure that potential risks posedby sharing Confidential data are identified,
documented and addressed according to this policy.Risk management plays an integral part in
the governance and management of the organizationat a strategic and operational level. The
purpose of a partner and third-party security policyis to ensure that partnerships and services
achieve their business plan aims and objectives, and are consistent withTellius, Inc.’s
requirements for information security.
Tellius, Inc.shall not share or transmit Confidentialdata to a third-party without first performing
a third-party risk assessment and fully executinga written contract, statement of work or
service agreement which describes expected servicelevels and any specific information security
requirements.
Third-Party Security Standards
7.
All third-parties must maintain reasonable organizationaland technical controls as assessed by
Tellius, Inc..
Assessment of third-parties which receive, process,or store Confidential data shall consider the
following controls as applicable based on the serviceprovided and the sensitivity of data stored,
processed or exchanged.
Information Security Policy
Third-parties maintain information security policiessupported by their executive management,
which are regularly reviewed.
Risk Assessment & Treatment
Third-parties maintain programs that assess, evaluate,and manage information and technology
risks.
Operations Security
Third-parties implement commercially reasonable practicesand procedures designed, as
appropriate, to maintain operations security. Protectionsmay include:
● Technical testing
● Protection against malicious software
● Network protection and management
● Technical vulnerability management
● Logging and monitoring
● Incident response
● Business continuity planning
3

Access Control
Third-parties maintain a technical access controlprogram.
Secure System Development
Third-parties maintain a secure development programconsistent with industry software and
systems development best practices including riskassessment, formal change management,
code standards, code review and testing.
Physical & Environmental Security
If third-parties are storing or processing confidentialdata, their physical and environmental
security controls should meet the requirements oftheTellius, Inc.Physical Security Policy.
Human Resources
Third-parties maintain human resource policies andprocesses which include criminal
background checks for any employees or contractorswho accessTellius, Inc.Confidential
information.
Compliance & Legal
Tellius, Inc.shall consider all applicable regulationsand laws when evaluating suppliers and third
parties who will access, store, process or transmitTellius, Inc.Confidential data. Third-party
assessments should consider the following criteria:
● Protection of customer data, organizational records,and records retention and disposition
● Privacy of Personally Identifiable Information (PII)
Exceptions
8.
Requests for an exception to this Policy must be submittedto theOperations Lead for approval.
Violations & Enforcement
9.
Any known violations of this policy should be reportedto the Operations Lead. Violations of this
policy can result in immediate withdrawal or suspensionof system and network privileges
and/or disciplinary action in accordance with companyprocedures up to and including
termination of employment.
